content:
# John Chapter 7 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 7 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 7:1 — "He would not go about in Judea" vs. "He deliberately avoided Judea"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he did not wish to walk in the Ioudaia, because the Ioudaioi were seeking to kill him" | Literal; "wish" / "walk" |
| LOGOS | "He would not go about in Judea, because the Jews were seeking to kill him." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "He deliberately avoided Judea because the Jewish leaders there were trying to kill him." | Dynamic; "deliberately avoided"; "leaders" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "deliberately avoided" adds intentionality not explicit in the Greek (οὐκ ἤθελεν...περιπατεῖν = "did not wish to walk"). PNEUMA again adds "leaders" to "Jews."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:2 — "Feast of Tabernacles" vs. "Festival of Shelters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the feast of the Ioudaioi was near, the Tabernacles" | Literal; transliterated |
| LOGOS | "the Jews' Feast of Tabernacles was at hand" | Formal; traditional name |
| PNEUMA | "the Jewish Festival of Shelters was approaching" | Dynamic; "Shelters" — modernized name |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "Feast of Tabernacles." This is the established English name for σκηνοπηγία. PNEUMA's "Shelters" is a valid translation but not the conventional term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:3-4 — "works" vs. "miracles" and "show yourself to the world" rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that also the disciples of you may see the works of you...show yourself to the world" | Literal; "works" |
| LOGOS | "that your disciples also may see the works you are doing...show yourself to the world" | Formal; "works" |
| PNEUMA | "where your followers can see the miracles you do!...show yourself to the world!" | Dynamic; "followers"; "miracles" for ἔργα |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. John uses ἔργα ("works"), not σημεῖα ("signs") or δυνάμεις ("mighty works/miracles"). The brothers speak of "works" — PNEUMA's "miracles" is interpretive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:6 — "My time has not yet come" vs. "Now is not the right time for me to go"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The time of me is not yet present, but the time of you is always ready." | Literal; "present" for πάρεστιν |
| LOGOS | "My time has not yet come, but your time is always here." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Now is not the right time for me to go, but for you any time is fine." | Dynamic; "right time"; "any time is fine" — colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. Note: the Greek here uses καιρός ("time/season"), not ὥρα ("hour"). PNEUMA's "any time is fine" is colloquial.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:12 — "much murmuring" vs. "widespread whispering" for Greek γογγυσμός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "much grumbling concerning him among the crowds" | Literal; "grumbling" |
| LOGOS | "much muttering about him among the people" | Formal; "muttering" |
| PNEUMA | "widespread whispering about him among the crowds" | Dynamic; "widespread whispering" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed SOPHIA consensus — "murmuring." γογγυσμός is a distinctive NT word (used of Israel's murmuring in the wilderness). "Murmuring" preserves this echo. LOGOS's "muttering" and PNEUMA's "whispering" lack this biblical resonance.

**Status:** Resolved using SOPHIA's own rendering. Both alternatives noted.

---

## 7:15 — "How does this man have learning?" vs. "How does he know so much?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "How does this [one] know letters, not having studied?" | Literal; "know letters" |
| LOGOS | "How is it that this man has learning, when he has never studied?" | Formal; "has learning" |
| PNEUMA | "How does he know so much when he's never been formally trained?" | Dynamic; "know so much"; "formally trained" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. EMET's "know letters" (γράμματα οἶδεν) is the most literal. LOGOS's "has learning" captures the institutional meaning. PNEUMA adds "formally" and "so much."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:17 — "If anyone's will is to do God's will" vs. "Anyone who truly wants to do God's will"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If anyone wills to do the will of him" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "If anyone's will is to do God's will" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who truly wants to do God's will can tell whether my teaching comes from God or whether I'm making it up." | Dynamic; adds "truly"; "I'm making it up" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "truly" (not in Greek) and replaces "speaking on my own authority" (ἀπ' ἐμαυτοῦ λαλῶ) with the colloquial "making it up."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:20 — "You have a demon!" rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You have a demon. Who seeks to kill you?" | Literal; flat declarative |
| LOGOS | "You have a demon! Who is seeking to kill you?" | Formal; exclamation |
| PNEUMA | "'You're demon-possessed!' the crowd retorted. 'Who's trying to kill you?'" | Dynamic; "demon-possessed"; "retorted" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "demon-possessed" is a modern clinical term that changes the register. The Greek simply says "you have a demon" (δαιμόνιον ἔχεις).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:21 — "I did one work" vs. "I did one miracle" for Greek ἓν ἔργον ἐποίησα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "One work I did" | Literal; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "I did one work" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "I did one miracle, and you're all stunned." | Dynamic; "miracle" for ἔργον; "stunned" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. Again, PNEUMA renders ἔργον as "miracle" — John's vocabulary distinction must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:24 — "judge with right judgment" vs. "start judging fairly"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "judge the righteous judgment" | Literal; cognate accusative |
| LOGOS | "judge with right judgment" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Stop judging by appearances, and start judging fairly." | Dynamic; "stop...start" imperative structure |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "start judging fairly" is colloquial and loses the formal "right/righteous judgment" (τὴν δικαίαν κρίσιν) — a legal term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:28 — "cried out" vs. "proclaimed" vs. "cried out"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Yeshua cried out in the temple, teaching and saying" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "So Jesus proclaimed, as he taught in the temple" | Formal; "proclaimed" for ἔκραξεν |
| PNEUMA | "Then Jesus, still teaching in the temple courts, cried out" | Dynamic; adds "still" and "courts" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed SOPHIA consensus — "cried out." ἔκραξεν means "shouted/cried out" — LOGOS's "proclaimed" is a slight elevation. "Temple courts" (PNEUMA) adds architectural specificity.

**Status:** Resolved with SOPHIA's own rendering.

---

## 7:38 — "Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water" vs. "rivers of living water will flow from deep within them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "rivers of living water will flow from the belly of him" | Literal; "belly" for κοιλίας |
| LOGOS | "Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water." | Formal; "heart" for κοιλία |
| PNEUMA | "rivers of living water will flow from deep within them" | Dynamic; "deep within them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "heart." κοιλία literally means "belly/womb/innermost part." "Belly" (EMET) is the most literal but sounds odd in English for spiritual discourse. "Heart" captures the sense of the person's inner being. PNEUMA's "deep within them" is acceptable but less specific.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:39 — "the Spirit was not yet" vs. "the Spirit had not yet been given"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for [the] Spirit was not yet, because the Yeshua was not yet glorified" | Literal; preserves Sinaiticus (no "given") |
| LOGOS | "for as yet the Spirit had not been given, because Jesus was not yet glorified" | Formal; adds "given" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "the Spirit had not yet been given, because Jesus had not yet entered into his glory" | Dynamic; adds "given"; "entered into his glory" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed SOPHIA consensus — "the Spirit was not yet" — preserving the Sinaiticus reading without the later scribal addition "given." This is theologically significant: John's bold statement is about the Spirit's new-covenant ministry, not the Spirit's existence. LOGOS and PNEUMA both add "given" which softens the Sinaiticus text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/Sinaiticus reading. LOGOS and PNEUMA noted as adding "given."

---

## 7:46 — "No one ever spoke like this man!" vs. "We have never heard anyone speak like this!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Never did a man speak thus." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "No one ever spoke like this man!" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "We have never heard anyone speak like this!" | Dynamic; shifts from speaking to hearing |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA shifts the focus from Jesus' speaking to the guards' hearing. The Greek (Οὐδέποτε ἐλάλησεν οὕτως ἄνθρωπος) is about the uniqueness of the speech.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:49 — "this crowd that does not know the law — they are accursed" vs. "This foolish crowd...God's curse is on them!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this crowd, the [one] not knowing the law — accursed they are" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "this crowd that does not know the law — they are accursed" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "This foolish crowd follows him, but they are ignorant of the law. God's curse is on them!" | Dynamic; adds "foolish"; "follows him"; "God's curse" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "foolish," "follows him," and attributes the curse to "God" — none of which are in the Greek. The Pharisees say the crowd is "accursed" (ἐπάρατοι), not specifically under "God's curse."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:51 — "Does our law judge a man without first giving him a hearing?" vs. "Is it legal to convict a man before he is given a hearing?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Does the law of us judge the man unless it first hears from him and knows what he does?" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Does our law judge a man without first giving him a hearing and learning what he does?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Is it legal to convict a man before he is given a hearing?" | Dynamic; "legal to convict" replaces "law judge"; omits "learning what he does" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA omits the second clause ("and learning what he does") and replaces "judge" with "convict" — which is a narrower legal term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words) | 10 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (restructuring) | 6 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure / transliteration | 6 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| "Works" vs. "miracles" (ἔργα) | 2 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Textual variant (Spirit "not yet" vs. "not yet given") | 1 | Resolved in favor of EMET/Sinaiticus |
| Festival name (Tabernacles vs. Shelters) | 1 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 7 reveals a growing pattern: PNEUMA tends to render Johannine theological vocabulary with modern equivalents that lose the precise biblical register — "miracles" for "works" (ἔργα), "Festival of Shelters" for "Feast of Tabernacles," "demon-possessed" for "you have a demon." The most significant textual-critical divergence is in v.39, where SOPHIA sides with EMET in preserving the raw Sinaiticus reading "the Spirit was not yet" rather than the softened "the Spirit had not yet been given" adopted by both LOGOS and PNEUMA. This is one of the few instances where SOPHIA overrides LOGOS in favor of EMET's literal fidelity.

The chapter's central theological moment — Jesus' cry on the last day of Tabernacles (vv.37-38) — draws strong agreement across all three translators, differing only in the rendering of κοιλία ("belly"/"heart"/"deep within").

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
